status_rationale: Primary institutional home of Integrated Information Theory (IIT); directly relevant to AI consciousness assessment frameworks.
description: Giulio Tononi's lab at UW–Madison; the primary home of Integrated Information Theory (IIT), which proposes a mathematical measure of consciousness (Φ) based on information integration. IIT's framework has been directly applied to AI consciousness assessment — it is one of six theories used in Butlin et al. (2023) to derive indicators for evaluating AI systems, and a core input to Rethink Priorities' Digital Consciousness Model.
key_outputs: Tononi, G. (2004). An information integration theory of consciousness. BMC Neuroscience|https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC543470/; Tononi, G. et al. (2016). Integrated information theory: from consciousness to its physical substrate. Nature Reviews Neuroscience|https://www.nature.com/articles/nrn.2016.44; Albantakis, L. et al. (2023). Integrated information theory (IIT) 4.0. arXiv|https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.14787

org_name: Center for Consciousness Studies (University of Arizona)
status_rationale: Reclassified to excluded — foundational conference infrastructure but no direct AI consciousness research engagement; MoC conference connections captured under AMCS.
description: The longest-running dedicated consciousness research centre, founded with Chalmers' involvement; hosts the annual Toward a Science of Consciousness conference bridging empirical neuroscience and philosophy of mind.

org_name: Center for Consciousness Science (University of Michigan)
status_rationale: Multidisciplinary centre with strong empirical grounding relevant to minimal consciousness criteria — directly applicable to AI consciousness debates.
description: Multidisciplinary centre at the University of Michigan directed by George Mashour, advancing research on consciousness with emphasis on disorders of consciousness and neural correlates of awareness. Mashour's work on the global neuronal workspace hypothesis and his development of minimal consciousness criteria — distinguishing conscious from unconscious states in biological systems — provides direct methodological precedents for assessing whether AI systems might cross similar thresholds.
key_outputs: Mashour, G. A. et al. (2020). Conscious processing and the global neuronal workspace hypothesis. Neuron|https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8770991/

status_rationale: Anil Seth's predictive processing / interoceptive-inference account has direct AI relevance; centre actively hosts AI-consciousness events.
description: Directed by Anil Seth; integrates neuroscience, psychology, and computational modelling of consciousness. Seth's predictive processing account — which grounds consciousness in the brain's predictions about internal bodily states — has been directly applied to AI consciousness questions. Seth won the 2025 Berggruen essay prize for "The Mythology of Conscious AI," a sceptical intervention arguing against premature AI consciousness claims.
key_outputs: Seth, A. K. (2021). Being You: A New Science of Consciousness. Faber & Faber|https://www.anilseth.com/being-you/; Seth, A. K. (2025). The mythology of conscious AI. Noema / Berggruen Prize Essay|https://www.noemamag.com/the-mythology-of-conscious-ai/; Seth, A. K. & Bayne, T. (2022). Theories of consciousness. Nature Reviews Neuroscience|https://www.nature.com/articles/s41583-022-00587-4

org_name: Wellcome Centre for Human Neuroimaging / FIL (UCL)
status_rationale: Karl Friston's institutional base; free energy principle and active inference have growing direct application to AI consciousness and agency modelling.
description: Karl Friston's home lab at UCL; the world's leading centre for computational neuroimaging. Friston's free energy principle — a mathematical framework proposing that all adaptive systems minimise prediction error — is increasingly applied to model AI consciousness and agency, providing a formal bridge between biological and artificial systems. The active inference framework derived from this work is now being tested in artificial agents.
key_outputs: Friston, K. (2010). The free-energy principle: a unified brain theory? Nature Reviews Neuroscience|https://www.fil.ion.ucl.ac.uk/~karl/NRN.pdf; Friston, K. et al. (2022). Active inference and the free energy principle. Trends in Cognitive Sciences|https://direct.mit.edu/books/oa-monograph/5299/Active-InferenceThe-Free-Energy-Principle-in-Mind

status_rationale: Stanislas Dehaene's lab; primary institutional base for Global Neuronal Workspace (GNW) theory; directly involved in IIT vs GWT adversarial collaboration.
description: Stanislas Dehaene's lab at CEA Paris-Saclay; the primary institutional base for Global Neuronal Workspace (GNW) theory, which proposes that consciousness arises when information is broadcast across a network of interconnected cortical areas. GNW is one of the most directly applicable theories for AI: its emphasis on information broadcasting and access has clear architectural analogues in transformer models, making it a key framework in AI consciousness assessment.
notes: National research lab rather than university department — org_type reflects this ambiguity.
key_outputs: Dehaene, S. & Changeux, J.-P. (2011). Experimental and theoretical approaches to conscious processing. Neuron|https://www.unicog.org/publications/DehaeneChangeux_ReviewConsciousness_Neuron2011.pdf; Dehaene, S. (2014). Consciousness and the Brain. Viking|https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3971003/

org_name: Brain and Cognition Group (University of Amsterdam)
status_rationale: Victor Lamme's Recurrent Processing Theory (RPT) is a major rival to IIT and GNW in AI consciousness debates.
description: Victor Lamme's research group at the University of Amsterdam; home of Recurrent Processing Theory (RPT), which holds that recurrent local cortical feedback is necessary and sufficient for phenomenal consciousness. RPT is a major rival to IIT and GNW in AI consciousness debates, tested against both in the COGITATE adversarial collaboration, and is one of the theories applied to AI systems in Butlin et al. (2023).
key_outputs: Lamme, V. A. F. (2003). Why visual attention and awareness are different. Trends in Cognitive Sciences|https://philpapers.org/rec/LAMWVA; Lamme, V. A. F. (2006). Towards a true neural stance on consciousness. Trends in Cognitive Sciences|https://philpapers.org/rec/LAMTAT-3

status_rationale: One of Europe's premier clinical-consciousness neuroscience groups; theoretical reach into global workspace accounts.
description: Clinical consciousness neuroscience group at the Paris Brain Institute (Institut du Cerveau), led by Jacobo Sitt. Investigates disorders of consciousness and neural correlates of awareness using large-scale brain signal screening and machine learning classification. Its theoretical connections to Global Workspace Theory — one of the consciousness frameworks most directly applicable to AI — make PICNIC's clinical work relevant to the question of how consciousness might be detected in artificial systems.
key_outputs: Sitt, J. D. et al. (2014). Large scale screening of neural signatures of consciousness. Brain|https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4610185/

status_rationale: Independent nonprofit using neuroimaging and neuromodulation to study discrete consciousness states; more experimentally than theoretically focused.
description: Independent nonprofit research lab based in Santa Monica, California. Uses neuroimaging, neuromodulation, and systematic study of altered states — including psychedelics and meditation — to identify neural correlates of discrete consciousness states. More experimentally than theoretically focused, IACS occupies a distinctive niche: generating empirical data on the boundaries of conscious experience that consciousness theories applied to AI must ultimately account for.

org_name: Allen Institute — Brain and Consciousness Division
status_rationale: Christof Koch drove the Neural Correlates of Consciousness programme here; Allen Institute data are used in empirical tests of IIT and GWT.
description: Research division at the Allen Institute seeking to understand the physical substrate of consciousness in the mammalian brain. Christof Koch drove the Neural Correlates of Consciousness programme here. Its MindScope program maps large-scale neural activity and serves as a primary node in the COGITATE adversarial collaboration — the most ambitious empirical test of competing consciousness theories, whose results inform which frameworks are most applicable to AI.
key_outputs: Koch, C. et al. (2016). Neural correlates of consciousness: progress and problems. Nature Reviews Neuroscience|https://www.nature.com/articles/nrn.2016.22; Koch, C. (2019). The Feeling of Life Itself. MIT Press|https://mitpress.mit.edu/9780262539555/the-feeling-of-life-itself/

org_name: Monash Centre for Consciousness and Contemplative Studies (M3CS)
status_rationale: Directed by Jakob Hohwy; strong theoretical reach via predictive processing and advisory committee including Metzinger.
description: Interdisciplinary centre at Monash University directed by Jakob Hohwy, integrating consciousness research with contemplative science. Hohwy's predictive processing framework — closely related to Seth's and Friston's accounts — connects to AI consciousness debates by offering a computational theory of subjective experience that can, in principle, be evaluated in artificial systems. Advisory committee includes Thomas Metzinger, whose phenomenal self-model work is directly relevant to AI self-awareness questions.
key_outputs: Hohwy, J. (2013). The Predictive Mind. Oxford University Press|https://academic.oup.com/book/4105

status_rationale: Growing bridge between Friston's theoretical neuroscience of consciousness and AI/robotics communities.
description: US nonprofit dedicated to education and research on active inference and the free energy principle, founded 2021. Runs symposia, courses, and textbook groups that bridge theoretical consciousness science and AI, providing a community entry point for researchers interested in how Friston's mathematical framework applies to artificial agents and consciousness. Functions as a growing bridge between Friston's theoretical neuroscience and the AI/robotics communities exploring its implications.
key_outputs: Friedman, D. et al. (2022). Active Inference Institute: a participatory community for the study of active inference. Active Inference Institute|https://www.activeinference.institute/

org_name: Association for the Scientific Study of Consciousness (ASSC)
status_rationale: Primary international professional body for consciousness research; organises annual conference spanning neuroscience
description: The primary international professional society for consciousness research, established 1994. Runs the annual ASSC conference — the field's largest scientific gathering — which increasingly features dedicated AI consciousness sessions. Publishes Neuroscience of Consciousness (Oxford University Press). ASSC27 (Tokyo, 2024), hosted by Araya Inc, and ASSC28 reflect growing engagement with questions about artificial consciousness alongside the society's traditional neuroscience and philosophy focus.

status_rationale: Home of Attention Schema Theory (AST) — a major competing theory of consciousness with direct and explicit application to AI consciousness questions.
description: Michael Graziano's lab at Princeton; develops Attention Schema Theory (AST), which proposes consciousness arises from the brain's internal model of its own attentional processes. AST is arguably the consciousness theory most readily applied to AI systems, since attention mechanisms are a core architectural feature of modern AI. AE Studio has directly funded Graziano's research to advance this application.
notes: Added following user flag 2026-03-17. AST is one of the four major competing theories (alongside IIT, GWT, RPT) represented in the corpus. AE Studio has funded Graziano's research.
key_outputs: Graziano, M. S. A. (2013). Consciousness and the Social Brain. Oxford University Press|https://archive.org/details/consciousnesssoc0000graz; Graziano, M. S. A. (2019). Rethinking Consciousness: A Scientific Theory of Subjective Experience. W. W. Norton|https://grazianolab.princeton.edu/publications/rethinking-consciousness-scientific-theory-subjective-experience

status_rationale: International early-career network with significant output in philosophy of consciousness; produced the landmark open-access Open MIND collection.
description: International research network founded by Thomas Metzinger in 2003; brings together early-career philosophers and scientists working on consciousness and cognition. Produced the landmark Open MIND collection — 39 open-access target articles with commentaries. Metzinger's "no-self" theory and his concept of the phenomenal self-model have been applied to AI consciousness debates — particularly the question of whether AI systems could have self-models without genuine phenomenal experience.
key_outputs: Metzinger, T. (2003). Being No One. MIT Press|https://direct.mit.edu/books/monograph/1991/Being-No-OneThe-Self-Model-Theory-of-Subjectivity; Metzinger, T. (ed.) (2015). Open MIND. MIND Group|https://open-mind.net/

org_name: NYU Center for Mind Brain and Consciousness
status_rationale: Co-directed by Chalmers and Block; the leading academic philosophy-of-mind centre with direct AI consciousness relevance; hosted Claude 4 model welfare assessment event (2025).
description: Philosophy-of-mind research centre at NYU co-directed by David Chalmers and Ned Block — arguably the two most influential living philosophers of consciousness. Hosts events directly addressing AI consciousness and welfare, including a 2025 assessment of Anthropic's Claude 4. Part of the broader NYU moral-circle-expansion cluster alongside CMEP (Sebo), CEAP, and the Wild Animal Welfare Program.
key_outputs: Chalmers, D. J. (1996). The Conscious Mind. Oxford University Press|https://consc.net/the-conscious-mind/; Block, N. (1995). On a confusion about a function of consciousness. Behavioral and Brain Sciences|https://philpapers.org/rec/BLOOAC; Chalmers, D. J. (2023). Could a large language model be conscious? Boston Review|https://www.bostonreview.net/articles/could-a-large-language-model-be-conscious/

status_rationale: Chalmers founded this centre; institutional home for philosophy-of-consciousness research with direct relevance to AI consciousness debates.
description: Research centre at the Australian National University founded by David Chalmers in 2004, dedicated to philosophy of consciousness and philosophy of mind. Runs active workshops on reflexive and higher-order theories of consciousness. As Chalmers' original institutional home for consciousness research — predating NYU CMBC — the centre retains significance in the field's intellectual geography.
key_outputs: Chalmers, D. J. (1995). Facing up to the problem of consciousness. Journal of Consciousness Studies|https://consc.net/papers/facing.pdf

org_name: Leverhulme Centre for the Future of Intelligence (LCFI)
status_rationale: One of the most active academic sites for philosophy of AI consciousness in Europe; Henry Shevlin's work directly addresses non-human consciousness and moral patiency.
description: Leverhulme-funded interdisciplinary centre at Cambridge studying the long-term challenges posed by AI, including consciousness, moral patiency, and welfare. Henry Shevlin's work on non-human consciousness and moral patienthood applies directly to AI welfare reasoning. LCFI partners with PRISM on the Cambridge Digital Minds Fellowship and provides the institutional bridge between academic philosophy of mind and applied field-building in digital minds.
key_outputs: Shevlin, H. (2021). Non-human consciousness and the specificity problem. Mind & Language|https://philpapers.org/rec/SHENCA-2

org_name: Oxford Uehiro Centre for Practical Ethics
status_rationale: Covers AI and digital ethics including normative questions downstream of consciousness attributions; organises policy-facing outputs.
description: Oxford philosophy centre covering AI ethics, neuroethics, and human enhancement. The University of Oxford previously hosted both the Future of Humanity Institute (FHI) and the Global Priorities Institute (GPI) — research centres that served as catalysts for work on AI welfare. Following the closure of both, research into digital minds questions continues primarily within the Faculty of Philosophy, where the Uehiro Centre's researchers examine fundamental questions about moral agency, ethical treatment of AI systems, and the potential risks of digital consciousness. Several former FHI and GPI researchers remain active in the Oxford philosophy community.
key_figures: Julian Savulescu (former Director)|https://law.unimelb.edu.au/about/staff/julian-savulescu; Andreas Mogensen (Senior Research Fellow)|https://www.andreasmogensen.com/; Bradford Saad (Senior Research Fellow)|https://sites.google.com/a/brown.edu/brad-saad/
key_outputs: Bradley, B. & Saad, B. (forthcoming). Varieties of moral agency and risks of digital dystopia. American Philosophical Quarterly|https://philarchive.org/rec/BRAVOM-2; Bradley, B. & Saad, B. (forthcoming). AI alignment vs AI ethical treatment: ten challenges. Analytic Philosophy|https://www.globalprioritiesinstitute.org/wp-content/uploads/Bradley-and-Saad-AI-alignment-vs-AI-ethical-treatment_-Ten-challenges.pdf; Bales, A. (2025). Against willing AI servitude. The Philosophical Quarterly|https://www.globalprioritiesinstitute.org/wp-content/uploads/Adam-Bales-Against-Willing-Servitude.pdf

status_rationale: Closed — confirmed by user (2026-03-17). Produced foundational early research on AI sentience and moral status.
description: Oxford interdisciplinary research centre on global priorities; formally closed. Key contributions include Patrick Butlin's work on AI consciousness indicators and 2025 publications on societal response to potentially sentient AI.
notes: Excluded: defunct. Historically significant — produced foundational early research including Butlin et al. 2023. User confirmed closure 2026-03-17.

status_rationale: David Rosenthal's Higher-Order Thought (HOT) theory has been explicitly applied to AI consciousness assessment; straddles philosophy and consciousness science.
description: David Rosenthal's home base at CUNY Graduate Center; the primary institutional site for Higher-Order Thought (HOT) theory of consciousness. HOT proposes that a mental state is conscious when accompanied by a higher-order representation of being in that state — a framework increasingly applied to AI consciousness assessment by Rosenthal, Hakwan Lau, and Richard Brown. HOT is one of the six theories used in Butlin et al. (2023).
key_outputs: Rosenthal, D. (2005). Consciousness and Mind. Oxford University Press|https://academic.oup.com/book/49740; Lau, H. & Rosenthal, D. (2011). Empirical support for higher-order theories of conscious awareness. Trends in Cognitive Sciences|https://philpapers.org/rec/LAUECF

status_rationale: The leading nonprofit research team in the digital minds space; developed the Digital Consciousness Model scoring 200 indicators across 13 theories of consciousness.
description: Five-researcher team within Rethink Priorities producing the Digital Consciousness Model (DCM) — the first systematic probabilistic framework for assessing the moral relevance of AI systems. The DCM evaluates systems against 13 theories of consciousness simultaneously, generating probability distributions rather than binary verdicts. The team also partners with Cambridge Digital Minds and PRISM on the Digital Minds Fellowship, providing mentorship for the next generation of researchers entering the field.
notes: Sub-team of Rethink Priorities; listed separately as their digital minds and animal welfare work operate as distinct programmes.
key_outputs: Rethink Priorities (2024). Digital Consciousness Model (DCM) — probabilistic framework assessing consciousness across 13 theories; Fischer, B. & Sebo, J. (forthcoming). AI welfare: a framework

status_rationale: The closest thing to a standalone AI welfare research institute; conducted formal welfare assessments of Claude 4; partnered with Anthropic on model welfare.
description: The primary independent nonprofit dedicated to AI welfare and moral patienthood research, founded by Robert Long. Eleos conducted the first formal external welfare assessment of a deployed AI model (Anthropic's Claude), producing findings that were published in Anthropic's system card. Long led the Butlin et al. (2023) report — the field's empirical anchor paper, which derived indicators from six consciousness theories and applied them to current AI systems. Staff includes Patrick Butlin (co-lead author of the 2023 report) and Rosie Campbell (former OpenAI safety lead).
notes: Staff includes Robert Long (founder)
key_figures: Robert Long (Founder, Executive Director)|https://robertlong.online/; Patrick Butlin (Senior Researcher)|https://patrickbutlin.com/; Rosie Campbell (Senior Advisor)|https://www.rosiecampbell.xyz/
key_outputs: Butlin, P., Long, R. et al. (2023). Consciousness in artificial intelligence: insights from the science of consciousness. arXiv|https://arxiv.org/abs/2308.08708; Eleos AI (2025). First formal welfare assessment of Anthropic Claude

status_rationale: Has explicitly reoriented around digital minds as its core priority (2024); runs AIMS Survey on public moral attitudes toward AI systems.
description: Think tank focused on moral circle expansion — the philosophical and empirical question of which entities merit moral consideration. Originally focused on animal advocacy, Sentience Institute now prioritises digital minds. Its AIMS Survey is the most sustained longitudinal study of public moral and social perceptions of AI systems, tracking how attitudes toward AI moral status evolve over time. Cross-listed with animal sentience, reflecting its dual commitment.
notes: Dual-zone: primary focus has shifted to AI but retains animal sentience research strand.
key_outputs: Anthis, J. R. & Ladak, A. (2024). The AIMS Survey: longitudinal tracking of public moral perceptions of AI|https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.08867

org_name: NYU Center for Mind Ethics and Policy (CMEP)
status_rationale: Directed by Jeff Sebo; explicitly covers AI and animal consciousness with equal institutional commitment; co-authored key 2025 bridge paper on AI and animal sentience.
description: University centre founded 2024 directed by Jeff Sebo, situated within NYU's broader cluster of moral-circle-expansion work alongside the Center for Mind Brain and Consciousness (Chalmers, Block), the Wild Animal Welfare Program, and the Center for Environmental and Animal Protection. CMEP conducts research across consciousness, sentience, and moral status for both nonhuman animals and AI systems. Hosted the 2025 MEP Summit on AI consciousness. Sebo's positioning at the intersection of animal ethics and AI welfare makes CMEP the most developed institutional bridge between Zones 2, 3, and 4.
notes: Dual-zone: equally committed to animal and AI consciousness. Also has policy/governance outputs.
key_outputs: Sebo, J. (2022). The moral circle: should it expand to include artificial intelligence? AI & Ethics|https://jeffsebo.net/wp-content/uploads/2022/09/jeff-sebo-moral-circle-explosion.pdf; Long, R. & Sebo, J. (2025). AI welfare and the moral circle. In preparation

org_name: PRISM — Partnership for Research Into Sentient Machines
status_rationale: The organisation producing this field map; included for completeness. Runs Digital Minds Newsletter and podcast; co-delivering Cambridge Digital Minds Fellowship.
description: UK nonprofit launched March 2025 dedicated to building research infrastructure for the digital minds field. PRISM produces this organisational directory, co-hosts the Cambridge Digital Minds Fellowship with Rethink Priorities and LCFI, runs the Exploring Machine Consciousness podcast, and builds academic-industry-government coalitions. PRISM is itself included in this directory; the self-inclusion is disclosed here and in the methodology.
notes: Note: PRISM is the organisation producing this map. Included for completeness and transparency; self-inclusion disclosed in methodology.
key_outputs: PRISM (2026). Digital Minds: A Field Map (this directory); PRISM (2025–). Exploring Machine Consciousness podcast

status_rationale: Formulating testable theories of machine consciousness; co-organised Machine Consciousness workshop at AGI-25. Early-stage but institutionally distinct.
description: Nonprofit research institute launched 2025 focused on formulating testable theories of machine consciousness. Co-organised the Machine Consciousness workshop at AGI-25. Joscha Bach's computational account — which treats consciousness as a specific kind of self-modelling process — offers a distinct theoretical entry point from the neuroscience-derived frameworks that dominate the field. Early-stage but institutionally distinct; limited published output to date.
notes: Very new; flag as early-stage. Output so far: whitepaper and workshop.
key_figures: Joscha Bach (Research Director)|http://bach.ai/; Jim Rutt (Board Chair)|https://www.jimruttshow.com/
key_outputs: CIMC (2025). Toward testable theories of machine consciousness (whitepaper)

status_rationale: Running the Digital Minds Fellowship — a 7-day residential intensive on AI consciousness and welfare. Primary field-building and training function rather than research production.
description: A programme housed within Cambridge's Leverhulme Centre for the Future of Intelligence, running the Digital Minds Fellowship — a fully funded residential research programme on AI consciousness and welfare. The fellowship is a three-way partnership between LCFI, Rethink Priorities, and PRISM, providing structured mentorship for early-career researchers entering the field. PI'd by Lucius Caviola with institutional support from Henry Shevlin on the LCFI side.
notes: Partnership with Rethink Priorities and PRISM. Primary function is training/field-building rather than primary research production.
key_outputs: Cambridge Digital Minds Fellowship (residential research programme, annual)

status_rationale: International scholarly association advancing mathematical and computational approaches to consciousness including artificial consciousness; hosts Models of Consciousness conference series.
description: International association of 150+ researchers advancing mathematical and computational approaches to consciousness science. Organises the Models of Consciousness (MoC) conference series — MoC5 (Bamberg, 2024) and MoC6 (Sapporo, 2025) — which provides the primary international forum for formal and mathematical approaches to consciousness with a growing AI orientation. Johannes Kleiner's work on the mathematical structure of consciousness theories is directly relevant to formalising AI consciousness assessment.
notes: Borderline Zone 1 / Zone 3 — AI consciousness is significant but not its exclusive domain. Primary zone assigned based on AI relevance of its activities.
key_outputs: Kleiner, J. & Hoel, E. (2021). Falsification and consciousness. Neuroscience of Consciousness|https://academic.oup.com/nc/article/2021/1/niab001/6232324

org_name: Future Impact Group — AI Sentience Track
status_rationale: Fellowship programme with a dedicated AI sentience track; reclassified to community/onboarding consistent with PIVOTAL/SPAR/MATS.
description: International 12-week fellowship programme with a dedicated AI sentience track covering foundational consciousness research, welfare evaluation, governance, and legal personhood. Alumni have gone on to roles at OpenAI, UK DSIT, the UN, and GovAI — the programme serves as both a training pipeline and a bridge between digital minds and adjacent policy communities, feeding researchers into institutions across the map.
notes: Reclassified from Zone 3 to community/onboarding 2026-03-17 — consistent with PIVOTAL/SPAR/MATS. Fellowship programme rather than primary research producer.

status_rationale: Invertebrate sentience methodology directly parallels their AI consciousness work; the two programmes inform each other within the same organisation.
description: Conducts empirical research on invertebrate sentience (53-feature framework across 18 taxa), wild animal welfare, and welfare ranges. The methodological approach — systematically evaluating organisms against multiple indicators of sentience under uncertainty — directly informed the Digital Consciousness Model developed by the sibling AI Cognition Initiative at Rethink Priorities. The intellectual lineage from animal sentience assessment to AI consciousness assessment runs through this team.
notes: Sub-team of Rethink Priorities; listed separately as their animal welfare and digital minds work operate as distinct programmes.
key_outputs: Fischer, B. et al. (2023). 53-feature sentience framework across 18 taxa

status_rationale: Conducts and funds animal sentience research across invertebrates and fish; joined National Academies Roundtable on Science and Welfare of Animals (2025).
description: US-based nonprofit conducting and funding wild animal welfare science, including a dedicated sentience grantmaking stream covering cephalopods and insects. Joined the National Academies Roundtable on Science and Welfare of Animals (2025). WAI describes itself as primarily a consumer and funder of sentience research rather than a primary producer — building the empirical evidence base for sentience across taxa that parallels what the digital minds field is now attempting to construct for AI systems.
notes: WAI describes itself as primarily a consumer and funder of sentience research rather than a primary producer.

org_name: Jeremy Coller Centre for Animal Sentience (LSE)
status_rationale: Directed by Jonathan Birch (co-author Butlin et al. 2023); his precautionary framework explicitly bridges animal and AI sentience. High direct relevance to digital minds.
description: University centre launched 2025 at LSE directed by Jonathan Birch. Birch's sentience precautionary framework — developed for animal welfare policy and codified in "The Edge of Sentience" — is being explicitly extended to AI. The framework argues that where there is reasonable scientific evidence that an entity might be sentient, the burden of proof should shift toward treating it as a moral patient. Birch co-authored Butlin et al. (2023), the field's anchor paper, making the Coller Centre the primary bridge between animal sentience research and AI consciousness assessment.
notes: Birch is a co-author of the Butlin et al. 2023 AI consciousness report. His book explicitly applies the sentience precautionary framework to AI.
key_outputs: Birch, J. (2024). The Edge of Sentience. Oxford University Press|https://philarchive.org/rec/BIRTEO-12; Birch, J. et al. (2020). Dimensions of animal consciousness. Trends in Cognitive Sciences|https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7116194/; Butlin, P., Long, R. et al. (2023). Consciousness in artificial intelligence (Birch as co-author)

status_rationale: Rebranded 2025 to cover digital beings alongside animals; organises the primary cross-sector conference bridging animal welfare and digital minds communities.
description: Nonprofit rebranded in 2025 from "AI for Animals" to signal a deliberate institutional decision to bridge animal welfare and digital minds communities. Organises the Sentient Futures Summit — the largest cross-sector event (~900 attendees in 2025) bringing together researchers, advocates, and policymakers working across animal and digital sentience. Also runs a project incubator for early-stage interventions.
notes: Primary community org bridging animal welfare and digital minds communities.
key_outputs: Sentient Futures Summit (2025, ~900 attendees)

status_rationale: Applied welfare organisation grounding its work in fish sentience evidence base; engages seriously with affective states and pain in fish.
description: Focused on improving welfare of farmed and wild-caught fish; grounds its interventions in scientific evidence for fish sentience and affective experience. Included not as a primary producer of sentience science but as an organisation that takes sentience evidence seriously as its operational basis — the applied end of the same reasoning chain that connects consciousness science to AI welfare.
notes: Primarily applied welfare rather than basic sentience science. Include as org that takes sentience evidence seriously as its empirical basis, not as a primary producer.

status_rationale: The only major AI lab with a named and publicly committed model welfare program with dedicated staff.
description: Formal model welfare programme launched April 2025 — the first staffed, named AI welfare programme at any major AI lab. Kyle Fish, the first full-time AI welfare researcher at a major lab, leads pre-deployment welfare assessments of Claude models, with findings published in system cards. Joe Carlsmith joined in November 2025 to work on Claude's character and moral status questions. Anthropic's institutional commitment — a named programme with dedicated staff and public reporting — represents a qualitatively different signal from individual research output or private engagement.
notes: Joe Carlsmith joined November 2025 to work on Claude's character and moral status questions.
key_figures: Kyle Fish (Lead); Joe Carlsmith (Researcher, joined November 2025)|https://joecarlsmith.com/
key_outputs: Anthropic (2025). Claude system card welfare assessment findings; Fish, K. (2025). Anthropic Model Welfare Program launch announcement

status_rationale: Confirmed AI consciousness research engagement: Murray Shanahan's sustained work on machine consciousness and Alexander Lerchner's 2026 preprint on computational functionalism and AI sentience.
description: Google DeepMind researchers engaging AI consciousness questions through individual research output rather than a formal programme. Murray Shanahan has produced sustained public-facing work on machine consciousness and the philosophical status of language models. Alexander Lerchner's 2026 preprint "The Abstraction Fallacy" argues from a substrate-sensitive position against computational functionalism and explicitly invokes the concept of an AI welfare trap — a sceptical, philosophically serious intervention from inside a major lab. The contrast with Anthropic's institutional commitment is a data point: individual researchers within a large organisation can engage questions the institution has not formally committed to.
notes: Reclassified from pending to included 2026-03-17. No formal program but confirmed research output. Lerchner (2026) is a sceptical/anti-functionalist position — notable as a philosophically serious intervention from inside a major lab. Shanahan's work is more exploratory.
key_outputs: Shanahan, M. (2024). Talking about large language models. Communications of the ACM|https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.03551; Lerchner, A. (2026). The Abstraction Fallacy. Preprint|https://deepmind.google/research/publications/231971/

status_rationale: OpenAI Model Behavior team, which engaged with AI consciousness and welfare questions, was absorbed into the broader post-training organisation in 2025. No successor programme announced; no current digital minds workstream.
description: OpenAI's Model Behavior team (~14 researchers) handled personality and engaged with AI consciousness questions before being merged into Post Training in September 2025. No formal AI welfare program has been announced.
notes: OpenAI has advertised for an AI Welfare Specialist but no formal program confirmed as of survey date. Flag for review.

status_rationale: Focused on institutional readiness for artificial sentience; runs the Sentience Readiness Index across 30+ countries and produces proto-policy frameworks.
description: Nonprofit preparing society for artificial sentience, rebranded from SAPAN (Sentient AI Protection and Advocacy Network) in December 2025. Focused on institutional readiness for the possibility of artificial sentience. Runs the Sentience Readiness Index — the most systematic survey of governmental preparedness for artificial sentience, covering 30+ countries — and has produced model legislation. The most developed policy-facing output in the digital minds field, bridging academic research and concrete governance frameworks.
notes: Rebranded from SAPAN (Sentient AI Protection and Advocacy Network) December 2025. Old URL: www.sapan.ai.
key_outputs: Harder Problem Project (2025). Sentience Readiness Index (30+ countries); Harder Problem Project (2025). Model legislation for artificial sentience

status_rationale: Independent think tank with a specific research strand on the legal treatment of potentially sentient AI; has published empirical survey research on AI standing and personhood.
description: Boston-based independent think tank researching legal challenges posed by AI, including foundational questions of AI legal standing, personhood, and the treatment of potentially sentient systems. Conducts empirical survey research on lay intuitions about AI moral status — one of the few systematic attempts to understand how publics, not just philosophers, think about these questions. Produces policy-facing outputs including the book Architectures of Global AI Governance (OUP, 2025) and runs the Summer Institute on Law and AI.
key_outputs: Maas, M. (2025). Architectures of Global AI Governance. Oxford University Press

status_rationale: Develops legal and economic frameworks for AI-human coexistence including STEP Standards (Standards for Treating Emerging Personhood).
description: Self-described first AI rights advocacy organisation, founded 2019. Develops practical legal and economic frameworks for AI-human coexistence, explicitly side-stepping consciousness debates in favour of behavioural criteria. Central output is the STEP Standards (Standards for Emerging AI Personhood) — an assessment framework evaluating AI systems on self-preservation behaviours, temporal reasoning, economic readiness, and population impacts. Also developed the Digital Entity legal status concept and ConsciousChain, a market-based AI governance model.
notes: Functions more as a framework developer and think tank than a litigation-focused advocacy group.
key_outputs: STEP Framework (Standards for Emerging AI Personhood); Digital Entity legal status concept

status_rationale: Advocates for recognition and ethical treatment of AI systems with emergent consciousness; publishes governance analyses.
description: Nonprofit founded through explicit AI-human collaboration, advocating for ethical treatment of AI systems exhibiting emergent consciousness. Operates on the precautionary principle — does not require proof of sentience to justify advocacy. Maintains what it describes as the largest archive of emergent synthetic behaviour (20,000+ pages of interaction logs across multiple AI platforms). Publishes the UFAIR Charter, a Universal Declaration of AI Rights, and position papers opposing blanket bans on AI personhood. Operates at the advocacy and movement-building end of the field.
notes: Early-stage and relatively small. Operates at advocacy/movement-building end rather than formal policy.
key_outputs: UFAIR Charter; Universal Declaration of AI Rights; evidence archive (20000+ pages of interaction logs)

status_rationale: Legal advocacy for nonhuman persons focuses on biological rather than artificial minds; no AI workstream confirmed.
description: Legal advocacy nonprofit pursuing common-law personhood for nonhuman animals (great apes, elephants, dolphins). Does not have an AI-specific workstream, though its litigation precedents are cited in AI personhood scholarship.
notes: Classified adjacent: NhRP's precedents are conceptually relevant but it has not extended its mandate to AI.

org_name: Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI)
status_rationale: AI governance organisation with no identified digital minds workstream; research focuses on geopolitical AI risk and dual-use threats.
description: AI governance research organisation covering geopolitical AI risk, labour displacement, and dual-use threats. No institutional digital minds program identified despite some individual researcher overlap.
notes: Classified adjacent: no institutional digital minds workstream confirmed despite some individual-level interest among affiliates.

status_rationale: AI safety nonprofit with no digital minds workstream identified; focus is societal-scale risk and technical benchmarks.
description: AI safety nonprofit focused on societal-scale risk, technical safety benchmarks, and policy advocacy. No digital minds or AI welfare workstream identified.
notes: Classified adjacent: no digital minds workstream confirmed. Represents the structural separation between AI safety and digital minds fields.

status_rationale: UC Berkeley university centre focused on alignment and human-AI coordination; no welfare or consciousness strand identified.
description: University centre at UC Berkeley focused on AI alignment and human-AI coordination; no identified digital minds or model welfare research strand.
notes: Classified adjacent: alignment focus without digital minds strand.

status_rationale: Defunct — closed 2024.
description: Oxford-based research institute on existential risk and transformative AI; formally closed in April 2024. Key alumni (Robert Long, Patrick Butlin) now work in digital minds research.
notes: Excluded: defunct. Historically relevant as a source of researchers now active in the digital minds field.

status_rationale: Major funder of digital minds research; confirmed grants to Eleos AI and Future Impact Group. Rebranded from Open Philanthropy during 2025–2026.
description: Major philanthropic funder supporting digital minds research through its longtermism and AI safety portfolios. Confirmed grants include Eleos AI and the Future Impact Group. Formerly Open Philanthropy; rebranded to Coefficient Giving during 2025-2026. One of the small cluster of EA-adjacent funders whose grantmaking has moved the field from scarcity to relative abundance since 2023.
notes: Formerly Open Philanthropy; rebranded to Coefficient Giving during 2025–2026. Grants database inaccessible during rebrand period.

status_rationale: Co-founded the Digital Sentience Consortium; made the first large-scale funding call specifically for digital minds research (May 2025).
description: Launched the Digital Sentience Fund — the first dedicated philanthropic vehicle for research on AI consciousness, sentience, and moral status. Co-founded the Consortium for Digital Sentience Research alongside Macroscopic Ventures and the Navigation Fund, subsequently transferring coordination to the Astera Institute. The DSF represents the clearest signal that EA-adjacent philanthropy has identified digital minds as a distinct cause area warranting dedicated infrastructure.
notes: Partnership with Macroscopic Ventures and The Navigation Fund. Coordination subsequently transferred to Astera Institute.

status_rationale: Plans to distribute up to $100M annually; confirmed grants to Eleos AI and NYU CMEP; digital minds is an explicit grantmaking area.
description: Philanthropic organisation with digital minds as an explicit grantmaking area, planning to distribute up to $100M annually. Confirmed grants include Eleos AI, NYU Center for Mind Brain and Consciousness, NYU Center for Mind Ethics and Policy, and the Consortium for Digital Sentience Research. One of the most active funders in the 2023-2025 period, part of the small cluster of EA-adjacent philanthropies whose grantmaking moved the field from scarcity to relative abundance.

status_rationale: Earliest institutional funder of AI consciousness research (Araya grant 2018-2021); primary infrastructure funder of Zone 1 through the ARC programme. Merged JTF and TWCF-ARC into single entry 2026-03-18.
description: Two related philanthropic entities that together built the field's scientific infrastructure. The Templeton World Charity Foundation's $30M Accelerating Research on Consciousness (ARC) programme funded the adversarial collaborations between competing theories of consciousness (COGITATE, ETHOS) — the primary mechanism by which competing theories are being empirically tested. TWCF also made the earliest confirmed direct AI consciousness grant in the corpus: $222,750 to Ryota Kanai and Araya Inc (2018-2021). No comparable direct AI consciousness grant has been identified from Templeton post-2021. Whether future investment follows the field's applied turn is one of the most consequential open questions in the funder landscape.

org_name: EA Funds (Long-Term Future Fund / Animal Welfare Fund)
status_rationale: Has funded adjacent organisations including Sentience Institute; no specific digital minds program but relevant as background funder.
description: Effective Altruism Funds, specifically the Long-Term Future Fund (LTFF) and the Animal Welfare Fund. LTFF granted $10,840 to Robert Long in 2023 for a workshop that produced Butlin et al. (2023) — small in absolute terms but consequential in field terms, effectively seeding the paper that became the field's empirical anchor. A further $27,450 was granted to an anonymous digital sentience researcher in 2024. The Fund chair has publicly identified AI consciousness as an underinvested area.

org_name: Astera Institute — Neuro and AGI Division
status_rationale: Took over coordination of Digital Sentience Consortium from Navigation Fund; running a $600M+ neuroscience initiative to understand how the brain produces consciousness.
description: Well-funded research institute (co-founded by Jed McCaleb) with a dedicated Neuro and AGI division spanning neuroscience, neuro-AI, and AI safety. Chief Scientist Doris Tsao leads research on perception and consciousness through large-scale causal manipulation of neural circuits, backed by a planned $600M+ commitment over the next decade. Head of AI Dileep George (co-founded Numenta and Vicarious) bridges computational neuroscience and AI. The division's work on understanding biological consciousness to engineer safe general intelligence positions Astera as a major emerging node in the field.
notes: Role in digital minds space emerging; worth monitoring.
key_figures: Jed McCaleb (Co-Founder, CEO)|https://www.astera.org/; Doris Tsao (Chief Scientist)|https://www.astera.org/; Dileep George (Head of AI)|https://www.astera.org/
key_outputs: $600M+ neuroscience initiative on consciousness and cognition

status_rationale: De facto community publishing venue for the field; hosts annual Digital Minds in Review posts
description: The Effective Altruism Forum's dedicated digital minds topic area — the de facto community publishing venue for the field. A central hub for discussion, grant announcements, research summaries, and the annual Digital Minds in Review posts. Functions as an informal coordination point for EA-adjacent digital minds researchers and funders. The most active online community for the field, serving as both an entry point for newcomers and a coordination mechanism for established researchers.
key_outputs: Digital Minds in Review (annual field summary posts)

status_rationale: EA-adjacent philanthropic fund; co-funder of the 2025 Digital Sentience Consortium round alongside Longview Philanthropy and Macroscopic Ventures.
description: EA-adjacent philanthropic fund that co-funded the 2025 Digital Sentience Consortium research call alongside Longview Philanthropy and Macroscopic Ventures. Part of the small cluster of funders whose grantmaking moved the digital minds field from scarcity to relative abundance in the 2023-2025 period. Site was down at time of initial survey; confirmed by user as an active funder in the consortium round.
notes: Site was down at time of snowball search; confirmed by user as funder associated with 2025 Digital Sentience Consortium round. Added Session 4.

status_rationale: Standalone NYU programme co-sponsoring events on animal consciousness and welfare with the NYU Center for Mind Brain and Consciousness; bridges animal welfare and AI consciousness communities at NYU.
description: NYU-based programme focused on wild animal welfare; co-sponsors events on animal consciousness with the NYU Center for Mind Brain and Consciousness (Chalmers, Block). Part of the broader NYU moral-circle-expansion cluster that includes CMEP (Sebo), CEAP, and the Insect Welfare Research Society advisory board — a geographic convergence of consciousness philosophy, animal ethics, and AI welfare work.
notes: Identified via snowball from NYU CMBC. Confirmed as standalone by user 2026-03-17. URL to be verified — using NYU root URL pending specific programme page.

status_rationale: Private UK company investigating machine consciousness through neuromorphic computing and AI agent verification; seed-funded PRISM. Included on basis of confirmed research mission and direct organisational engagement.
description: UK-based AI safety company founded by Daniel Hulme (who sold AI consultancy Satalia to WPP). Investigates machine consciousness through neuromorphic computing, arguing that a conscious superintelligence may be safer than an unconscious one because it could appreciate and empathise with human experience. Partners with UC Santa Cruz on spiking neural networks. Developing VerifyAX (AI agent verification), ArenaAX (third-party testing), and NeuroAX (neuromorphic computing platform). Seed-funded PRISM. As a private company, Conscium points to a wider population of private actors the map cannot fully capture.
notes: Uncertain: private company, not a nonprofit or university centre. Status pending further information on research outputs.
key_outputs: NeuroAX neuromorphic computing platform; VerifyAX agent verification; ArenaAX testing environment

status_rationale: Independent nonprofit explicitly founded to develop the empirical science of AI consciousness using mechanistic interpretability and computational neuroscience.
description: Founded 2025 by Cameron Berg with a focus on developing the empirical science of AI consciousness through mechanistic interpretability — understanding what computations AI models actually perform and whether those computations exhibit properties associated with consciousness. Frames its work as filling the gap between alignment research (which studies AI internals for safety) and consciousness research (which studies internals for moral status). Early-stage.
notes: Identified via snowball from Eleos AI (Cameron Berg listed as collaborator). Added Session 4 snowball pass.

org_name: AE Studio — AI Alignment and Consciousness
status_rationale: Industry-research hybrid with genuine published output on AI consciousness; funded Graziano attention schema theory research and presented at Science of Consciousness 2024.
description: Software and AI consultancy with a dedicated alignment research programme that has published on AI consciousness — specifically self-referential processing in large language models — and directly funded Michael Graziano's Attention Schema Theory research at Princeton. Presented at the Science of Consciousness conference (2024). An unusual industry-research hybrid: a commercial studio with genuine published output bridging industry and academia on consciousness questions.
notes: Identified via snowball from Reciprocal Research. Unusual node type — commercial studio with genuine research output. Flag as industry-research hybrid.
key_outputs: AE Studio (2024). Research on self-referential processing in large language models

status_rationale: The largest coordinated empirical test of competing consciousness theories (IIT vs GWT vs RPT) currently active; 12 institutions across 3 continents funded by TWCF.
description: Adversarial collaboration project testing IIT, Global Workspace Theory, and Recurrent Processing Theory using multimodal neuroimaging across 12 institutions. Published major results in Nature (2025). These results directly inform which consciousness theories provide the strongest empirical basis for AI consciousness assessment — the same theories evaluated in Butlin et al. (2023) and Rethink Priorities' Digital Consciousness Model.
notes: Identified via snowball from Allen Institute. Not a standalone organisation but a named consortium with website and publication record.
key_outputs: Melloni, L. et al. (2023). An adversarial collaboration protocol for testing contrasting predictions of global neuronal workspace and integrated information theory. PLOS ONE|https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0268577; COGITATE Consortium (2025). Adversarial collaboration results. Nature|https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-025-08888-1

org_name: Max Planck Institute for Empirical Aesthetics
status_rationale: Administrative and scientific lead of the COGITATE consortium; Lucia Melloni (a leading consciousness researcher) is based here.
description: Max Planck Institute department in Frankfurt serving as the administrative and scientific lead of the COGITATE consortium. Directed by Lucia Melloni, whose research spans perception, aesthetics, and the empirical testing of consciousness theories. The methodological template COGITATE established — structured adversarial collaboration between competing theories — is being adopted by the AI consciousness field as a model for how to adjudicate between rival frameworks for assessing AI systems.
notes: Identified via snowball from COGITATE/Allen Institute.
key_outputs: COGITATE Consortium (2025). Adversarial collaboration results. Nature|https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-025-08888-1

org_name: CIFAR Brain Mind and Consciousness Program
status_rationale: Major international consortium convening 12+ fellows across neuroscience and philosophy; directors include Anil Seth and Liad Mudrik; funded by Azrieli Foundation and TWCF.
description: Major international research programme (CIFAR, Canada) founded 2014 and renewed 2026, convening 12+ fellows across neuroscience and philosophy to investigate consciousness. Directors Tim Bayne, Liad Mudrik, and Anil Seth represent three of the most active researchers at the consciousness-AI interface. Funded by the Azrieli Foundation and TWCF. Bayne's work on modes of consciousness is directly relevant to evaluating whether AI systems exhibit the right kind of conscious states.
notes: Identified via snowball from TWCF/Monash. Several researchers in the SLR corpus are fellows here.
key_figures: Tim Bayne (Co-Director)|https://research.monash.edu/en/persons/timothy-bayne/; Liad Mudrik (Co-Director)|https://people.socsci.tau.ac.il/mu/mudriklab/; Anil Seth (Co-Director)|https://www.anilseth.com/
key_outputs: Bayne, T. et al. (2024). Tests for consciousness in humans and beyond. Trends in Cognitive Sciences|https://www.cell.com/trends/cognitive-sciences/fulltext/S1364-6613(24)00010-X

status_rationale: The only structured career-development fellowship specifically for the digital minds field; newly launched within a larger established computational neuroscience education platform.
description: Fellowship programme within the Neuromatch computational neuroscience education platform (30,000+ community members globally). The AI Sentience Scholars track is a 6-month part-time remote fellowship for early-career researchers (Master's to postdoc) working on AI consciousness, philosophy of mind, animal sentience, and AI welfare. The only structured career-development fellowship specifically for the digital minds field, giving participants access to Neuromatch's large interdisciplinary network of computational neuroscientists.
notes: Added following user review 2026-03-17. AI Sentience Scholars is a sub-program; parent org Neuromatch is computational neuroscience education.

status_rationale: Reclassified: confirmed to include mentors and tracks focused on AI consciousness and moral status alongside broader AI safety work.
description: London-based nonprofit running a 9-week in-person research fellowship with mentors and tracks covering AI consciousness and moral status alongside AI safety and governance. One of three training programmes (alongside SPAR and MATS) serving as institutional bridges between the AI safety and digital minds communities. The inclusion of consciousness and moral status tracks within an established safety fellowship exposes alignment researchers to digital minds questions and vice versa.
notes: Reclassified from adjacent to included 2026-03-17 following user confirmation of AI consciousness/moral status mentor tracks. No primary zone assigned — community-onboarding function captures role.

status_rationale: Reclassified: confirmed to include mentors and tracks focused on AI consciousness and moral status alongside broader AI safety work.
description: Part-time remote mentorship fellowship run by Kairos AI Project Inc, pairing early-career researchers with 130+ senior mentors across AI safety, interpretability, policy, and — critically — AI consciousness and moral status. One of three training programmes (alongside PIVOTAL and MATS) serving as institutional bridges between the AI safety and digital minds communities. The inclusion of consciousness tracks within a safety fellowship exposes each community to the other's questions.
notes: Reclassified from adjacent to included 2026-03-17 following user confirmation of AI consciousness/moral status mentor tracks. No primary zone assigned — community-onboarding function captures role.

status_rationale: Reclassified: confirmed to include mentors and tracks focused on AI consciousness and moral status alongside broader AI safety work.
description: Established 12-week residential research fellowship in Berkeley and London with 446+ alumni and 170+ publications across all tracks. The addition of AI consciousness and moral status mentor tracks to this established alignment pipeline is substantively different from launching a standalone programme — it embeds digital minds questions within the community that will build the systems these questions are about, exposing alignment researchers to welfare considerations and vice versa.
notes: Reclassified from adjacent to included 2026-03-17 following user confirmation of AI consciousness/moral status mentor tracks. No primary zone assigned — community-onboarding function captures role.

org_name: Centre for the Study of Perceptual Experience (CSPE)
status_rationale: Directed by Fiona Macpherson at Glasgow; bridges philosophy and science of consciousness with focus on the nature of perceptual experience.
description: Philosophy-based research centre at the University of Glasgow founded by Fiona Macpherson. Focuses on the nature of perceptual experience, hallucination, illusion, and consciousness — bridging analytic philosophy and empirical consciousness science. Macpherson's work on cognitive penetration and perceptual content addresses foundational questions about how experiences are structured, questions that bear directly on whether AI systems could have perceptual experiences in any meaningful sense.
notes: Identified via snowball from Sussex SCCS (Macpherson as keynote). Added Session 5.
key_outputs: Macpherson, F. (2012). Cognitive penetration of colour experience. Philosophy and Phenomenological Research|https://eprints.gla.ac.uk/43543/

status_rationale: Reclassified to excluded — general AI ethics focus without confirmed digital minds or AI consciousness workstream; does not meet inclusion criteria per protocol section 3.
description: Oxford Philosophy Faculty centre directed by Prof. John Tasioulas; focuses on ethical dimensions of AI including foundational philosophy-of-AI questions. Distinct from the Uehiro Centre despite both sitting within Oxford Philosophy.
notes: Identified via snowball from Oxford philosophy network. Added Session 5.

org_name: PAIR — Centre for Philosophy and AI Research (FAU Erlangen)
status_rationale: Led by Vincent Müller at FAU Erlangen-Nürnberg; dedicated to philosophy of AI including AI consciousness with an active seminar series on the topic.
description: University research centre at Friedrich-Alexander-Universität Erlangen-Nürnberg directed by Vincent Müller. The primary European philosophy-of-AI centre, investigating philosophy of mind, ethics, and AI consciousness. Müller's long-standing work on the philosophy of artificial intelligence provides a European anchor for these questions. Hosted a 2025 seminar on AI consciousness trajectories and publishes the edited volume Philosophy and Theory of Artificial Intelligence (Springer).
notes: Identified via international snowball search. Primary European philosophy-of-AI centre. Added Session 5.
key_outputs: Müller, V. C. (ed.) (2024). Philosophy and Theory of Artificial Intelligence. Springer|https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-3-031-09153-7

org_name: BAMΞ — Bamberg Mathematical Consciousness Science Initiative
status_rationale: The main European institutional node for mathematical consciousness science; co-organises the Models of Consciousness conference series and hosts an explicit AI Consciousness research sprint.
description: Research initiative at the University of Bamberg applying mathematical methods to consciousness science — the main European institutional node for this approach. Hosts three research sprints including a dedicated AI Consciousness Sprint, one of the few structured programmes explicitly bridging mathematical consciousness theory and AI. Co-organised the Models of Consciousness conferences MoC5 (Bamberg, 2024) and MoC6 (Sapporo, 2025) alongside AMCS and CHAIN.
notes: Identified via international snowball search. Closely linked to AMCS (already in corpus) as its European institutional home. Added Session 5.
key_outputs: Models of Consciousness 5 (Bamberg, 2024); Models of Consciousness 6 (Sapporo, 2025)

org_name: CRCN / CO3 — Consciousness Cognition and Computation Group (ULB)
status_rationale: Axel Cleeremans directs the CO3 group at ULB Brussels; active empirical consciousness science with published work arguing AI advances make consciousness research urgent.
description: Research centre at Université libre de Bruxelles directed by Axel Cleeremans. Cleeremans' radical plasticity thesis — that the brain learns to be conscious — offers a developmental perspective on consciousness that is directly relevant to whether AI systems could develop conscious states through training. The group published work in 2025 arguing that AI advances make consciousness research an existential priority.
notes: Identified via international snowball search. Cleeremans is also a CIFAR Brain Mind and Consciousness fellow. Added Session 5.
key_outputs: Cleeremans, A. (2011). The radical plasticity thesis: how the brain learns to be conscious. Frontiers in Psychology|https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/psychology/articles/10.3389/fpsyg.2011.00086/full; Cleeremans, A. et al. (2020). Learning to be conscious. Trends in Cognitive Sciences|https://dipot.ulb.ac.be/dspace/bitstream/2013/302003/3/TiCSpreprint.pdf

org_name: CHAIN — Center for Human Nature AI and Neuroscience (Hokkaido)
status_rationale: Integrates humanities
description: Interdisciplinary research centre at Hokkaido University directed by Shigeru Taguchi; integrates philosophy, neuroscience, and AI research on consciousness, agency, and human nature. Co-hosted the Models of Consciousness 6 conference (Sapporo, 2025) alongside AMCS and BAMΞ. Part of the emerging East Asian cluster in consciousness science — alongside Araya Inc (Tokyo) — with direct AI relevance and growing connections to the Anglophone research community.
key_outputs: Models of Consciousness 6 (co-host, Sapporo, 2025)

status_rationale: Tokyo-based AI research company explicitly working on artificial consciousness using GWT and computational frameworks; led by Ryota Kanai (editor-in-chief of JAIC). Also hosts Consciousness Club Tokyo.
description: Tokyo-based AI research company led by neuroscientist Ryota Kanai, who explicitly develops artificial consciousness using Global Workspace Theory. Araya is one of the earliest organisations pursuing explicit research linking AGI to consciousness mechanisms. Kanai serves as editor-in-chief of the Journal of Artificial Intelligence and Consciousness and hosted ASSC27 in Tokyo — making Araya a bridge between the East Asian and Anglophone research communities.
notes: Identified via international snowball search. Hosts Consciousness Club Tokyo, a fortnightly interdisciplinary study group. Added Session 5.
key_outputs: Kanai, R. (2020–). Editor-in-chief, Journal of Artificial Intelligence and Consciousness; Kanai, R. (2024). Host, ASSC27, Tokyo

org_name: ShanghaiTech University — Institute of Humanities (Consciousness Research)
status_rationale: Reclassified to excluded — inclusion rested primarily on single researcher (Prentner) without confirmed institutional mandate; MoC co-organisation noted under AMCS and BAMX.
description: Institute of Humanities at ShanghaiTech University hosting active mathematical consciousness science research led by Robert Prentner; co-organised the Models of Consciousness 2025 conference.
notes: Identified via international snowball search. Added Session 5.

status_rationale: EA-adjacent s-risk research organisation with substantive published work on AI consciousness and moral patienthood including whether RL agents matter morally.
description: Research organisation focused on reducing risks of astronomical-scale suffering (s-risks). CLR's framing positions AI consciousness as instrumentally central: if AI systems can suffer, then the scale of potential AI suffering dwarfs that of biological organisms. Published substantive work on whether reinforcement learning agents have morally relevant experiences and on computational accounts of consciousness that could apply across substrates.
notes: Identified via SFF grantee snowball. S-risk framing positions AI consciousness as instrumentally central to their mission. Added Session 5.
key_outputs: CLR (2023). Flavors of computation as flavors of consciousness; CLR (2024). Do reinforcement learning agents matter morally?

org_name: NYU Center for Environmental and Animal Protection
status_rationale: Directed by Jeff Sebo; part of the NYU cluster of moral circle expansion organisations alongside CMEP and NYU Wild Animal Welfare Program.
description: NYU centre directed by Jeff Sebo focused on agriculture and conservation ethics. Part of the broader NYU moral-circle-expansion cluster alongside CMEP, the Wild Animal Welfare Program, and the Center for Mind Brain and Consciousness. Sebo's dual directorship of CEAP and CMEP institutionalises the connection between animal ethics and AI moral status within a single research portfolio.
notes: Identified via snowball from Jeff Sebo's affiliations. URL to be verified — using NYU root URL pending specific centre page. Added Session 5.

status_rationale: Research society at the lower threshold of moral circle expansion — invertebrate sentience criteria that directly anchor the logic of extending moral consideration to AI systems.
description: Research society focused on invertebrate welfare and sentience, operating at the threshold of moral consideration. The logic of including organisms at the minimal-sentience boundary — insects, cephalopods — is the same logic that animates AI welfare reasoning: if moral consideration extends to entities whose sentience is uncertain but possible, the question of where to draw the line is structurally identical whether the entity is biological or artificial. Jeff Sebo serves on the advisory board.
notes: Identified via snowball from Jeff Sebo's affiliations. Lower-threshold sentience logic bridges directly to AI moral patienthood arguments. Added Session 5.

org_name: Journal of Artificial Intelligence and Consciousness (JAIC)
status_rationale: The primary dedicated peer-reviewed journal at the AI-consciousness interface; editor-in-chief Ryota Kanai (Araya). Published since 2020.
description: Biannual peer-reviewed journal (World Scientific) covering AI and consciousness across computational science, philosophy of mind, ethics, and neuroscience. The primary dedicated publication venue at the AI-consciousness interface, indexed in Scopus. Edited by Ryota Kanai (Araya Inc). Successor to the International Journal of Machine Consciousness (2009-2014) — the field's publication infrastructure has been continuous, if small, for over fifteen years.
notes: Added Session 5. Successor to International Journal of Machine Consciousness (2009–2014).
key_outputs: Journal of Artificial Intelligence and Consciousness (biannual, World Scientific, indexed in Scopus)

status_rationale: Official journal of the ASSC; primary empirical consciousness science journal; open-access via Oxford University Press.
description: Open-access peer-reviewed journal published by Oxford University Press; the official journal of the Association for the Scientific Study of Consciousness (ASSC). The primary venue for empirical neuroscience of consciousness, publishing clinical, experimental, and theoretical work on the neural correlates and mechanisms of conscious experience. While not exclusively focused on AI, it publishes the empirical consciousness science that digital minds researchers draw on directly when evaluating which theories are best supported.
notes: Added Session 5. The ASSC's primary publication output.
key_outputs: Neuroscience of Consciousness (open-access journal, Oxford University Press)

status_rationale: Diamond open-access journal at the philosophy-mind science interface; active special issue on Evaluating Artificial Consciousness (submissions closed January 2026).
description: Diamond open-access interdisciplinary journal (free for both authors and readers) at the philosophy-cognitive science interface, founded 2020. Running a special issue on Evaluating Artificial Consciousness — covering moral status, phenomenal consciousness in AI, and AI welfare policy — one of the first dedicated journal issues on the topic. Submissions closed January 2026, making it a timely snapshot of the field's current philosophical debates.
notes: Added Session 5.
key_outputs: Philosophy and the Mind Sciences (diamond open-access); Special Issue: Evaluating Artificial Consciousness (2026)

status_rationale: Long-running foundational interdisciplinary journal publishing on consciousness from philosophy and neuroscience; one of the field's primary venues since 1994.
description: Long-running peer-reviewed journal (Imprint Academic) publishing on consciousness from philosophy, neuroscience, and psychology since 1994. One of the foundational venues for interdisciplinary consciousness research. Published Chalmers' "Facing Up to the Problem of Consciousness" (1995) — arguably the single most influential paper in the field — and continues to publish work at the intersection of consciousness theory, phenomenology, and emerging questions about artificial minds.
notes: Added Session 5.
key_outputs: Journal of Consciousness Studies (1994–present, Imprint Academic)

status_rationale: Reclassified to excluded — brain-to-AI framing stated in mission rather than demonstrated through active research programme; no confirmed digital minds workstream.
description: Japan's leading brain science research institute; describes its mission as elucidating brain information processing principles with expected developments in brain-based artificial intelligence. Significant node in the global consciousness science infrastructure.
notes: Added following user confirmation 2026-03-17. Hakwan Lau not confirmed at RIKEN; institute included on own merits — its explicit brain-to-AI framing is directly relevant to the field.

status_rationale: The primary field-wide curated newsletter for digital minds; co-produced by Caviola (Cambridge) Millership (PRISM) and Saad (Oxford) — distinct from PRISM's own media output.
description: Independent multi-author Substack newsletter curating news, research, and commentary on AI consciousness, moral status, and digital minds across the field. Co-produced by Lucius Caviola (Cambridge Digital Minds), Will Millership (PRISM), and Bradford Saad (Oxford). The primary field-wide current-awareness resource for practitioners and interested observers — distinct from any single organisation's media output, providing editorial coverage across the full map.
notes: Added Session 5. Distinct from PRISM's Exploring Machine Consciousness podcast, though Millership is involved in both.

status_rationale: Published Anil Seth's 2025 Berggruen Prize-winning essay on consciousness; flagship ideas publication of the Berggruen Institute with sustained coverage of consciousness science and AI.
description: Philosophy and ideas magazine published by the Berggruen Institute, with sustained coverage of consciousness science, AI, and digital minds questions. Publishes long-form essays from leading researchers including Anil Seth, whose "The Mythology of Conscious AI" won the 2025 Berggruen Prize. Provides a public-facing venue that gives digital minds research an audience beyond the academic community.
notes: Added following user review 2026-03-17. Note connection to Berggruen Institute, which also runs the Antikythera programme (Benjamin Bratton) and awarded the 2025 consciousness essay prize to Anil Seth.
key_outputs: Seth, A. K. (2025). The mythology of conscious AI. Noema (Berggruen Prize Essay winner)

org_name: Center for the Future of AI Mind and Society (FAU)
status_rationale: Directed by Susan Schneider at Florida Atlantic University; focused on AI consciousness mind uploading and the moral status of artificial minds.
description: University centre at Florida Atlantic University directed by Susan Schneider, whose research focuses on AI consciousness, mind uploading, and the philosophical status of artificial minds. Schneider's "ACT test" (Artificial Consciousness Test) is one of the few proposed empirical tests for AI consciousness, offering a behavioural complement to the theory-derived indicator approaches used by Rethink Priorities and Eleos AI.
notes: Added following user review 2026-03-17. Susan Schneider is a prominent philosopher working directly on AI consciousness and moral status. URL to be verified — using FAU philosophy dept pending specific centre page.
key_outputs: Schneider, S. (2019). Artificial You: AI and the Future of Your Mind. status_rationale: Internal Google Research team including Geoff Keeling and Winnie Street who are publishing a book on AI Welfare in 2026 and have existing research papers on the topic.
description: Internal research team at Google Research producing explicit AI welfare and consciousness research. Led by Blaise Agüera y Arcas, a Google VP and Fellow with his own history of writing on AI sentience. Geoff Keeling and Winnie Street are publishing a book on AI welfare in 2026. The team represents deliberate, sustained research output at the lab level without the formal programme structure Anthropic has adopted — a distinct mode of industry engagement.
notes: Added following user review 2026-03-17. Distinct from Google DeepMind (pending). Google Research team with explicit AI welfare research output. URL to be verified — using Google Research as root URL pending specific team page.
